entities.description: Public electronic text derived from selected matter in the 1909 EETS issue: its title page, the embedded 1887 Chaucer Society title page, and the edited poem on printed pp. 1–120. It omits the edition's foreword and introduction, Clouston's abstract, notes, glossary, maps, and other back matter.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00044

rights.basis: U-M identifies the source materials as public domain and displays a No Copyright–United States/DPLA rights signal; institutional terms and non-U.S. rights remain separate.
rights.component: U-M electronic text and public item representation
rights.jurisdiction: United States

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall and W. G. Stone, eds., The Tale of Beryn, with a Prologue of the Merry Adventure of the Pardoner with a Tapster at Canterbury, EETS Extra Series 105 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society, 1909).
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 105
bibliographic.title: The Tale of Beryn, with a Prologue of the Merry Adventure of the Pardoner with a Tapster at Canterbury

entities.description: EETS Extra Series 105 issue of the Chaucer Society text. Its pages were printed from casts of the earlier plates and retain the 1887 title page after the new EETS title page. Furnivall and Stone transcribed the unique Northumberland witness and compared it with Urry's 1721 print; Furnivall's bracketed interventions include supplied letters, words, phrases, and several conjectural lines.
entities.label: Furnivall and Stone, The Tale of Beryn (1909 EETS issue)

physicalDescription.extent: xii + 238 pages, with inserted pages 200a–200b and plates
notes: CME00044 represents the two title pages and poem on pp. 1–120, not the complete volume.

rights.basis: The 1909 publication and its nineteenth-century plate source are in the U.S. public domain; scan derivatives and provider terms are separate.
rights.component: 1909 and reused nineteenth-century edited text, introduction, apparatus, and arrangement
rights.jurisdiction: United States

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall and W. G. Stone, eds., The Tale of Beryn, Chaucer Society, Second Series 17 and 24 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1876–1887; title page dated 1887).
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner & Co. for the Chaucer Society
bibliographic.title: The Tale of Beryn, with a Prologue of the Merry Adventure of the Pardoner with a Tapster at Canterbury

entities.description: The earlier plate issue from which the 1909 EETS text was reproduced. Cataloging describes a publication span of 1876–1887, while the title page embedded in the EETS volume is dated 1887. Furnivall's foreword is dated 13 August 1888, so publication and paratext dates are preserved without forcing a single undifferentiated date.
entities.label: Chaucer Society edition of The Tale of Beryn (1876–1887; title page 1887)

bibliographic.citation: John Urry, ed., The Works of Geoffrey Chaucer, Compared with the Former Editions, and Many Valuable MSS. (London: Bernard Lintot, 1721).
bibliographic.title: The Works of Geoffrey Chaucer, Compared with the Former Editions, and Many Valuable MSS.

entities.description: The first printed edition of the Beryn Prologue and Tale, prepared from the Northumberland manuscript then lent by the Hon. Mrs. Thynne. Furnivall and Stone consulted and collated Urry's text but also worked directly from the manuscript.
entities.label: John Urry, The Works of Geoffrey Chaucer (1721)

rights.basis: Published in 1721, centuries before modern U.S. copyright terms.
rights.component: 1721 edited text, typography, arrangement, and historical edition content
rights.jurisdiction: United States

access.accessMethod: Apply to the Archivist for an appointment. The 2024 guide describes Wednesday and Thursday access from 1 April through 23 October, subject to approval and conservation constraints.
access.entityLabel: Alnwick Castle, Collection of the Duke of Northumberland, MS 455
access.format: Onsite consultation of the physical manuscript
notes: The 2024 guide quotes £55 per research day and £50 per day for a camera permit, including five captures; current charges should be confirmed before travel.
access.provider: Alnwick Castle Archive, Collection of the Duke of Northumberland

access.accessMethod: Request a quotation for complete-object photography. The standard in-house service is capped at 30 captures, so the entire codex requires a larger or external order by arrangement.
access.entityLabel: Alnwick Castle, Collection of the Duke of Northumberland, MS 455
notes: The 2023 sheet quotes a £40 administration fee plus per-image charges of £3 per double-page spread, £5 per single image up to A4, £8 up to A1, and £14 above A1, with a possible £30 large-item processing fee. A complete codex would require a bespoke quotation.

Request the entire present manuscript object, not only the Beryn leaves: covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, both front paper flyleaves, all 287 parchment leaves including blanks and adjacent Canterbury Tales material, both rear paper flyleaves, and edges or other physical views when offered.
In the resulting complete delivery, associate CME00044 only with the work_portion ff. 180r–235r. Preserve all surrounding manuscript images as whole-object context.
Ask the archive to confirm current foliation, image sequence, color targets, resolution, file format, completeness, and whether any surfaces cannot be photographed.

Supplied images are restricted to noncommercial research and private study unless the archive gives separate written permission for reproduction, reuse, redistribution, publication, or Internet display.
access.provider: Alnwick Castle Archive, Collection of the Duke of Northumberland

rights.basis: Alnwick's image-request terms limit supplied images to noncommercial research and private study and prohibit supplying them to others or reproducing, reusing, redistributing, publishing, or placing them on the Internet without prior written permission.
rights.component: Existing or commissioned photographs supplied by Alnwick Castle Archive
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and any jurisdiction specified in supplied terms
notes: A complete-object research order may be cached privately, but Git publication, print-on-demand use, or other commercial or public reuse requires explicit written permission covering that use.

access.accessMethod: No public complete digital facsimile or complete image inventory was verified. Use the repository enquiry route or consult the 2009 physical facsimile.
access.entityLabel: Alnwick Castle, Collection of the Duke of Northumberland, MS 455
access.format: No public complete digital object found
notes: This is a dated negative finding, not a categorical claim that MS 455 has never been digitized.
LMES exposes only f. 180r; the Senshu surrogate is a physical printed volume; the official British Library microfilm access list omits MS 455.
access.provider: Alnwick Castle Archive, Collection of the Duke of Northumberland

entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of the Prologue and Tale of Beryn, which occupies ff. 180r–235r within a parchment Canterbury Tales codex. LMES dates the manuscript 1450–1475, attributes the Beryn portion to its Beryn Scribe, and localizes that scribe's language to LALME Linguistic Profile 6040 in Essex. This witness-level dialect evidence is not treated as proof that the work was composed in Essex.

holding.notes: No exact repository-owned public child catalog record for MS 455 was established. LMES provides the exact scholarly description; Alnwick's archive page provides repository access information.

entities.label: Alnwick Castle, Collection of the Duke of Northumberland, MS 455

physicalDescription.dimensions: Approximately 308 × 198 mm; written space approximately 220 × 100–110 mm
physicalDescription.extent: Two front paper flyleaves, 287 parchment textblock leaves, and two rear paper flyleaves
physicalDescription.format: Illuminated manuscript codex; quires principally of eight
notes: LMES records red and blue paraphs and blue lombard initials with red penwork.
The Beryn work occupies ff. 180r–235r.
The exact historical relationship between legacy MS 55 and current MS 455 remains to be documented, but the sources use them for the same physical witness.
physicalDescription.support: Parchment textblock with modern paper flyleaves

rights.basis: The medieval text predates modern copyright; this does not extend to modern photographs, transcriptions, editorial matter, or contractual access terms.
rights.component: Underlying fifteenth-century Middle English and Latin textual content
rights.jurisdiction: United States

rights.basis: Alnwick's image-request terms limit supplied images to noncommercial research and private study and prohibit supplying them to others or reproducing, reusing, redistributing, publishing, or placing them on the Internet without prior written permission.
rights.component: Existing or commissioned photographs supplied by Alnwick Castle Archive
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and any jurisdiction specified in supplied terms
notes: A complete-object research order may be cached privately, but Git publication, print-on-demand use, or other commercial or public reuse requires explicit written permission covering that use.

next_steps: Retain anonymous responsibility and separate tentative component dates rather than assigning the work to Chaucer.
Evaluate Pearsall's c. 1410 proposal for the Tale, Brown's 1420 jubilee context for the Interlude, and arguments for a Canterbury or Christ Church connection from the cited scholarship.
Treat the Latin colophon's ‘son of the church of St Thomas’ as association evidence, not a personal name.
openQuestions.question: Who composed the two Beryn components, and were the Tale and Canterbury Interlude written at different dates?

next_steps: Ask Alnwick Archive for shelfmark concordance or recataloging history.
Preserve MS 55 and Nthld 55 as legacy designations of MS 455 until documentary evidence explains the change.
Do not create a second witness entity from the number difference alone.
openQuestions.question: Why do Furnivall, MED, and older scholarship call the witness MS 55 while current catalogs identify it as MS 455?

next_steps: Request a bespoke Alnwick quotation for covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all four paper flyleaves, all 287 parchment leaves including blanks and adjacent works, and edges where offered.
Ask for an exact image inventory and identify ff. 180r–235r only as CME00044's work_portion within the full codex delivery.
Inspect the Senshu volume at NDL or BL MS Facsimile 2228 and record whether it includes bindings, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, color targets, and edges.
Obtain written terms separately for private cache retention, Git redistribution, scholarly publication, and commercial or print-on-demand reuse.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete digital reproduction of the entire present MS 455 object be obtained, and exactly which physical surfaces does the 2009 printed facsimile reproduce?

next_steps: Align Furnivall/CME against Bowers and the f. 180r image without assuming that every italic or bracketed span represents the same manuscript feature.
Separate the 4,024 English lines and two Latin colophon lines from one apparatus quotation and two tail-note continuation lines included in the raw XML L count.
Use a complete manuscript facsimile, if obtained, to classify expansions, supplies, deletions, abbreviations, and layout evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which specific Furnivall supplies and line divisions account for the 4,026 work lines represented in CME00044 versus Bowers's 4,022-line section?

subject.description: Generic archive access, reproduction, publication, and microfilm guidance

relations.subjectLabel: Alnwick Castle Archive access, imaging, and microfilm guidance

reviewNotes: Current shelfmark MS 455 and the legacy forms MS 55 or Nthld 55 are modeled as designations of one witness, not as separate manuscripts.

A whole-object request means covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, two front and two rear paper flyleaves, all 287 parchment leaves including blanks and adjacent works, and edge views when offered. The Beryn work portion is then identified separately as ff. 180r–235r.
The 2009 Senshu facsimile is cataloged as a full-manuscript printed surrogate, but public records do not establish whether it reproduces every non-textblock surface.

The LMES image is a selected view of f. 180r, not a complete digital manuscript. It was not downloaded because LMES and holding-library terms limit manuscript-image use to private study absent written permission.
The British Library's official Alnwick microfilm list includes adjacent shelfmarks but omits MS 455; this is negative evidence only and does not prove that no microfilm exists anywhere.

The work-line total of 4,026 in the Furnivall/CME representation includes a two-line Latin colophon; Bowers prints a 4,022-line modern text. The differing editorial lineations are retained rather than silently reconciled.

The repository cache contains complete downloadable editions and complete access-policy documents, not a manuscript facsimile. Cache coverage labels apply to each downloaded source object, not to MS 455.

lineage.summary: CME00044 is the repository XML representation of the two-part Middle English Prologue and Tale of Beryn, including the 1909 and 1887 title pages and edited pp. 1–120 of Furnivall and Stone's EETS volume. The 1909 issue reused plates from the Chaucer Society edition, whose title page is dated 1887 and which directly transcribed the unique witness now cataloged as Alnwick Castle, Collection of the Duke of Northumberland, MS 455; the edition also consulted John Urry's first printing of 1721. The work occupies ff. 180r–235r of the complete 287-leaf parchment textblock. No public complete digital facsimile of MS 455 was found. One LMES image of f. 180r is viewable for private study and deliberately remains uncached. A 2009 Senshu University printed facsimile represents the complete manuscript as a separate physical surrogate, while Alnwick offers onsite access and priced reproduction by arrangement. Any reproduction request should cover the entire present codex and all available physical surfaces, then map ff. 180r–235r only as the Beryn work portion.

catalogingSubject.label: The Prologue and Tale of Beryn
catalogingSubject.scope_note: A connected, anonymous two-part Middle English verse unit: a 732-line Canterbury Interlude or Prologue followed by the Merchant's Tale of Beryn. The two components share a unique witness and editorial transmission but may have different composition dates. CME00044 represents both components and their two-line Latin colophon, not the complete 1909 source volume.

contentParts.label: Canterbury Interlude or Prologue

locators.value: begins f. 180r and ends before the Tale begins on f. 190r; exact final Interlude side should be verified from a full facsimile

notes: The first 36 lines precede Furnivall's printed leaf marker ‘180 back’; comparison with Bowers and LMES confirms that the work begins on f. 180r, not f. 180v.
contentParts.summary: The 732-line continuation of the Canterbury pilgrimage frame, centered on the Pardoner's comic misadventure with the tapster Kit.

contentParts.label: Merchant's Tale of Beryn

locators.value: pp. 25–120; 3,292 English lines plus two Latin colophon lines

notes: Two quoted continuation lines from the Summoner's Tale occur in the XML tail note after the Beryn colophon and are excluded from this component's 3,294-line count.
contentParts.summary: The adapted merchant romance and legal satire, ending with 3,292 English verse lines and a two-line Latin colophon in the Furnivall/CME representation.

dateStatements.display: Early fifteenth century; the components may have been composed separately
notes: The normalized range is deliberately broad. It is a work-composition estimate, not the date of the surviving manuscript.

dateStatements.display: The Tale has been proposed to date to about 1410
notes: Bowers reports Pearsall's proposal; the range encodes approximate dating without claiming precision.

dateStatements.display: The Interlude has been associated with Canterbury's jubilee year of 1420
notes: Historical association does not establish an exact composition year.

dateStatements.display: The unique surviving witness is dated 1450–1475
notes: A witness-production date is a terminus for attestation, not the same thing as the work's composition date.

dateStatements.display: First printed in John Urry's Works of Geoffrey Chaucer in 1721

extent.description: Two connected verse components in the Furnivall/CME representation: a 732-line Interlude and a 3,294-line Tale unit, including the Tale's two-line Latin colophon. Five LG containers and 4,026 work-level L elements are encoding-layer counts, not a universal critical lineation; Bowers prints 4,022 lines.

formStatements.description: Narrative verse in rhyming couplets; the final two-line Latin colophon is also verse.

genres.label: Chaucerian continuation
notes: A continuation in the Canterbury Tales tradition, not a work by Chaucer.

genres.label: Fabliau
notes: The Pardoner and tapster episode uses comic sexual trickery characteristic of fabliau; this label is component-scoped.

genres.label: Translation and adaptation
notes: The English Tale substantially condenses and reshapes the first part of the French prose Bérinus.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: A two-line Latin colophon follows the 3,292 English Tale lines in the Furnivall/CME representation.
CME00044 catalogs a two-part medieval work represented through an edited selection, not the complete 1909 scholarly volume.
The preferred title is conventional and supplied. Title pages, edition titles, component labels, incipits, and manuscript rubrication should remain distinguishable.
The anonymous work is pseudo-Chaucerian in transmission and framing; Geoffrey Chaucer is not assigned author responsibility.
The French Bérinus relationship applies to the Tale, not to the Canterbury Interlude.
The final two Latin lines are part of the Tale's colophon. Three other L elements in XML notes are excluded from work-level verse counts.
LMES's Essex localization describes the Beryn Scribe's witness language and is not silently transferred to the work's composition region.
MS 455 and legacy MS 55/Nthld 55 are one physical witness pending exact shelfmark-history evidence.
Facsimile coverage, full-object request scope, exact work-portion locators, downloaded-source status, image rights, and publication restrictions are maintained in the paired lineage manifest.

next_steps: Review the primary arguments behind the c. 1410 Tale and c. 1420 Interlude proposals.
Preserve anonymous authorship unless direct evidence supports a named candidate.
Do not convert a possible Christ Church or Canterbury association into a precise composition place.
openQuestions.question: Were the Canterbury Interlude and Tale composed by the same anonymous poet, at what dates, and how should the colophon's St Thomas affiliation be interpreted?

next_steps: Align both editions against a complete manuscript facsimile if one is obtained.
Classify each Furnivall bracketed or italic intervention instead of treating all formatting as abbreviation expansion.
Keep English poem lines, Latin colophon lines, apparatus quotations, and tail-note quotations in separate counting layers.
openQuestions.question: Which editorial supplies and line divisions account for the Furnivall/CME 4,026-line work total versus Bowers's 4,022-line section?

next_steps: Keep witness dialect localization separate from work composition.
Review linguistic and institutional arguments for Canterbury, Christ Church, and southeast England.
Retain England as the conservative work-level region until stronger evidence is evaluated.
openQuestions.question: What composition locale, if any, can be supported independently of the narrative's Canterbury setting and MS 455's Essex-localized Beryn Scribe?

next_steps: Request or inspect the entire manuscript object rather than only ff. 180r–235r.
Within the complete object, map ff. 180r–235r as the Beryn work_portion and retain adjacent Canterbury Tales material as codex context.
Record bindings, pastedowns, all four flyleaves, blanks, every parchment leaf, edges, color targets, image sequence, and omitted surfaces.
Obtain written permission before Git publication or commercial reuse of any supplied modern images.
openQuestions.question: What complete-codex visual evidence can be obtained for MS 455, and which physical surfaces are included in the Senshu printed facsimile or a future Alnwick image order?

notes: England is a conservative cultural origin. It does not claim a precise place of composition.
The Interlude recounts the pilgrims' Canterbury stay and the Pardoner's tavern adventure; narrative setting is not composition place.
The St Thomas colophon, pilgrimage setting, and scholarly arguments support a southeastern or Canterbury association, but not a secure exact composition locale.
No authorship is assigned to Geoffrey Chaucer. The work is a fifteenth-century continuation associated with the Canterbury Tales tradition.
The Tale adapts and condenses the first part of the French prose Bérinus; adapter and author are retained as distinct roles.
Editor of the represented Chaucer Society text and its 1909 EETS plate issue; his bracketed supplies are substantial and should not be confused with medieval authorship.

summaries.value: A pseudo-Chaucerian continuation of the Canterbury Tales in two connected verse components. The Interlude extends the pilgrims' Canterbury stay with a comic account of the Pardoner's attempted affair with the tapster Kit. The following Tale recounts the merchant Beryn's youthful dissipation, sea voyage, victimization by fraudulent townspeople, and rescue through the ingenuity of Geoffrey, adapting the first part of the French prose Bérinus. The unique Alnwick witness closes with a two-line Latin colophon associating the translator with the church of St Thomas.

The pilgrims lodge at Canterbury, visit the shrine, and pass time before departure. The Pardoner pursues the tapster Kit but is tricked and humiliated in a comic sexual adventure before the company turns to the next tale.

After wasting his inheritance through gambling, Beryn goes to sea as a merchant. In a corrupt foreign city he is trapped by a series of fraudulent claims, but the resourceful Geoffrey exposes the schemes, reverses the legal contest, and enables Beryn's restoration and repentance.

The CME XML contains 4,029 L elements overall, but only 4,026 belong to the work containers: 732 in the Interlude and 3,294 in the Tale, the latter comprising 3,292 English lines plus two Latin colophon lines. One quoted line in an apparatus note and two quoted continuation lines in the tail note explain the raw-document surplus. Bowers's modern edition uses a different 4,022-line lineation.

notes: A concise conventional title for the connected two-part work; not claimed as an original manuscript title.

The Interlude extends the pilgrims' Canterbury stay with a comic account of the Pardoner's attempted affair with the tapster Kit. The following Tale recounts the merchant Beryn's youthful dissipation, sea voyage, victimization by fraudulent townspeople, and rescue through the ingenuity of Geoffrey, adapting the first part of the French prose Bérinus. The unique Alnwick witness closes with a two-line Latin colophon associating the translator with the church of St Thomas.
